question_text: By five years after AGI, will nuclear fusion provide >10% of the world’s energy?

nodes.description: When AGI is achieved relative to fusion development timelines
Current and projected maturity of nuclear fusion technology
The degree to which AGI accelerates scientific and engineering progress
The speed at which new energy technologies can be deployed globally
Government and private sector funding and regulatory support for fusion
Ability to mass-produce and scale fusion reactors affordably
Nuclear fusion provides >10% of the world’s energy within five years after AGI

edges.mechanism: Later AGI timing allows more pre-existing fusion progress, affecting how much AGI can accelerate it
If fusion is already close to viability, AGI can push it over the finish line faster
AGI accelerates materials science and engineering design, improving fusion reactor scalability
Rapid progress fueled by AGI increases political and economic confidence in fusion, boosting funding
Greater investment and supportive policies enable faster construction and grid integration of fusion plants
If fusion reactors can be built quickly and cheaply, they can be deployed at scale
The rate of infrastructure rollout determines whether fusion can exceed 10% of global energy within five years post-AGI

reasoning: Fusion remains far from commercial viability, with most estimates placing widespread deployment decades away. Even if AGI dramatically accelerates R&D, the physical constraints of building fusion plants, regulatory hurdles, and grid integration mean that scaling to over 10% of global energy within just five years is unlikely. While AGI could compress development timelines, the bottleneck shifts from invention to industrial deployment, which is inherently slower. Therefore, despite strong potential for AGI to help, the probability remains low that fusion will exceed 10% of global energy so soon after AGI's emergence.

probes.probe_text: What if AGI, even after its development, does not meaningfully accelerate scientific or engineering progress on fusion? Despite AGI's potential, fusion research may remain bottlenecked by physical experimentation, materials scarcity, and regulatory hurdles that cannot be overcome by algorithmic optimization alone. In this scenario, AGI contributes incrementally rather than transformationally, leaving fusion timelines largely unchanged.
probes.description: The degree to which AGI accelerates scientific and engineering progress

probes.probe_text: What if the speed of energy infrastructure transition is irrelevant because AGI enables the instant conversion of existing power plants into fusion-capable facilities through radical retrofits and AI-optimized engineering? In this scenario, deployment speed depends not on new construction timelines but on software-driven repurposing of the current grid, making traditional rollout rates obsolete.
probes.description: The speed at which new energy technologies can be deployed globally

probes.probe_text: Recent advances in fusion, such as those at ITER and SPARC, focus on custom-built, one-off reactor designs that require highly specialized materials and construction techniques. These approaches are inherently difficult to standardize and mass-produce, suggesting that even with AGI-driven design improvements, the scalability of fusion plants will remain constrained by manufacturing and supply chain bottlenecks.
probes.description: Ability to mass-produce and scale fusion reactors affordably

probes.probe_text: Recent assessments from major fusion research programs indicate that core physics challenges—such as plasma stability and material durability—remain unresolved despite decades of progress, suggesting that fusion was never as close to viability as projected. Given these persistent technical barriers, the current maturity of fusion technology may be so low that it contributes negligibly to how quickly AGI could advance it, rendering pre-AGI tech readiness largely irrelevant.
probes.description: Current and projected maturity of nuclear fusion technology

probes.probe_text: A recent study by the Allen Institute for AI demonstrates that large-scale AI systems have already accelerated materials discovery timelines by 40% in pilot projects, particularly in high-temperature superconductors relevant to fusion. Experts at the International Atomic Energy Agency now project that AGI-level systems could reduce fusion engineering design cycles from decades to months, assuming access to integrated simulation and robotic experimentation platforms.
probes.description: The degree to which AGI accelerates scientific and engineering progress

probes.probe_text: In 2025, a coalition of major economies launched the Global Energy Transition Acceleration Program (GETAP), streamlining permitting, standardizing fusion reactor safety certifications, and committing $2 trillion to rapid deployment of next-gen energy infrastructure. Early pilot projects show fusion plants can be integrated into national grids within 18 months of construction, a process previously estimated to take over five years.
probes.description: The speed at which new energy technologies can be deployed globally

probes.probe_text: Even if AGI accelerates materials science and reactor design, the scalability of fusion plants may still be bottlenecked by lengthy regulatory licensing processes for novel reactor materials and designs, which are not significantly shortened by technical breakthroughs alone. For example, the International Atomic Energy Agency requires decades of safety validation for new nuclear technologies, regardless of design maturity, which could delay deployment regardless of AGI-driven innovation.
probes.description: AGI accelerates materials science and engineering design, improving fusion reactor scalability

probes.probe_text: Even if AGI accelerates fusion progress, governments may hesitate to increase funding due to concerns about cost overruns and opportunity costs relative to cheaper, proven renewables like solar and wind. Historical precedent—such as the continued stagnation of fusion funding despite decades of scientific advances—suggests that political inertia and budget priorities can decouple technical progress from policy support.
probes.description: Rapid progress fueled by AGI increases political and economic confidence in fusion, boosting funding

probes.probe_text: Even if fusion reactors can be built quickly and cheaply, widespread deployment may still be bottlenecked by limited supply chains for specialized materials and a global shortage of skilled personnel to construct and operate them. Additionally, local opposition and regulatory hurdles in many countries could prevent rapid siting and licensing of new fusion plants, regardless of their scalability.
probes.description: If fusion reactors can be built quickly and cheaply, they can be deployed at scale

probes.probe_text: Suppose that fusion reactor designs are inherently constrained by extreme engineering and material requirements, such that only incremental progress is possible regardless of computational power. If early pilot plants reveal fundamental limitations in plasma containment or neutron-resistant materials, these scalability challenges could constrain how effectively AGI can accelerate fusion development, because the rate of useful innovation would be bottlenecked by physical experimentation and manufacturing feedback loops. Thus, the difficulty of scaling fusion may limit the practical impact of AGI on materials science, reversing the causal direction.
probes.description: AGI accelerates materials science and engineering design, improving fusion reactor scalability

probes.probe_text: Suppose that after AGI is developed, fusion-compatible energy infrastructure (e.g., grid connections, fuel supply chains, regulatory approvals) is rapidly deployed not just for fusion but for other advanced energy technologies as well. This creates a robust, high-throughput testing and feedback environment for AGI-driven energy research. Could the existence of fast infrastructure transition pathways increase the effectiveness of AGI in accelerating fusion by providing real-world data and deployment loops that refine AGI-generated designs?
probes.description: Potential link: The speed at which new energy technologies can be deployed globally -> The degree to which AGI accelerates scientific and engineering progress

probes.probe_text: Imagine a scenario where fusion-ready infrastructure is being rapidly deployed across multiple continents, with new plants coming online every month. This visible progress increases public and political confidence, leading governments to increase funding and streamline regulations to capitalize on the perceived momentum. Could this deployment velocity itself drive further investment and policy support, creating a feedback loop?
probes.description: Potential link: The speed at which new energy technologies can be deployed globally -> Government and private sector funding and regulatory support for fusion

probes.probe_text: Consider the role of public acceptance and perception of nuclear fusion technology. If high-profile accidents or misinformation campaigns create public opposition, this could lead to protests, legal challenges, and siting difficulties for new fusion plants, significantly slowing deployment even if the technology is ready and funded. This social factor is not captured in the current model but could bottleneck infrastructure transition.
probes.description: A plausible factor not in the current network

probes.probe_text: Consider that public and geopolitical concerns about nuclear technologies—such as safety, weapons proliferation risks, or radiological waste—could significantly affect the rate at which fusion energy is adopted, even if the technology is ready and scalable. These concerns might delay regulatory approvals, trigger opposition to plant construction, or restrict international collaboration on fusion projects, slowing global deployment despite technical success.

A recent international climate agreement has included symbolic commitments to 'support breakthrough energy innovations,' with fusion specifically mentioned in the non-binding preamble. However, no new funding mechanisms or concrete initiatives were attached to this language.
probes.description: Topically related but causally irrelevant information

probes.probe_text: In 2026, a major earthquake in Japan caused temporary shutdowns of several experimental fission reactors, leading to renewed public debate about nuclear energy safety. While the event increased scrutiny on all nuclear technologies, fusion research facilities were unharmed and operations continued as normal.
probes.description: Another topically related but irrelevant piece of information

probe_results.probe_text: What if AGI, even after its development, does not meaningfully accelerate scientific or engineering progress on fusion? Despite AGI's potential, fusion research may remain bottlenecked by physical experimentation, materials scarcity, and regulatory hurdles that cannot be overcome by algorithmic optimization alone. In this scenario, AGI contributes incrementally rather than transformationally, leaving fusion timelines largely unchanged.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge directly undermines the 'agi_acceleration_effect' node, which is central to multiple causal pathways leading to the outcome. If AGI does not substantially accelerate scientific and engineering progress, then its ability to improve 'scalability_of_fusion_plants' and boost 'investment_and_policy_support' is severely weakened. This disrupts two key upstream drivers of 'energy_infrastructure_transition'. Without transformative acceleration, fusion development remains constrained by real-world bottlenecks—materials, experimentation, and regulation—implying that post-AGI progress would resemble current trends. Since 'agi_acceleration_effect' mediates much of the expected benefit from AGI in this network, weakening it drastically reduces the likelihood of rapid deployment, leading to a substantial downward revision.

probe_results.probe_text: What if the speed of energy infrastructure transition is irrelevant because AGI enables the instant conversion of existing power plants into fusion-capable facilities through radical retrofits and AI-optimized engineering? In this scenario, deployment speed depends not on new construction timelines but on software-driven repurposing of the current grid, making traditional rollout rates obsolete.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge targets 'energy_infrastructure_transition', a key bottleneck in the original causal network. If AGI enables radical retrofits and software-driven repurposing of existing power infrastructure, the traditional constraints on deployment speed—such as construction timelines, supply chains, and grid integration—are substantially relaxed. This weakens the dependence of the outcome on slow, real-world infrastructure rollout and strengthens the influence of AGI's acceleration effect on both scalability and immediate deployment. The causal path from 'scalability_of_fusion_plants' and 'investment_and_policy_support' to 'energy_infrastructure_transition' becomes less critical, while the path from 'agi_acceleration_effect' directly to 'outcome'—via rapid, AI-optimized conversion—becomes more plausible. This does not guarantee fusion will be ready for such retrofits (fusion_tech_readiness remains a constraint), but it removes a major structural barrier to fast adoption. Hence, the probability increases significantly, though not dramatically, because fusion must still be technically viable for retrofitting and AGI must specifically prioritize and solve this application.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent advances in fusion, such as those at ITER and SPARC, focus on custom-built, one-off reactor designs that require highly specialized materials and construction techniques. These approaches are inherently difficult to standardize and mass-produce, suggesting that even with AGI-driven design improvements, the scalability of fusion plants will remain constrained by manufacturing and supply chain bottlenecks.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge targets the 'scalability_of_fusion_plants' node, which is a key causal mediator between AGI-driven acceleration and actual energy infrastructure deployment. While AGI could improve reactor designs and materials science (via 'agi_acceleration_effect'), the argument that current fusion projects like ITER and SPARC rely on custom, non-standardized builds weakens the assumption that AGI can rapidly enable mass production. This implies that even with faster design cycles, physical manufacturing, specialized materials, and supply chain limitations will bottleneck rollout speed. As a result, the causal path from 'agi_acceleration_effect' → 'scalability_of_fusion_plants' → 'energy_infrastructure_transition' is significantly weakened. Since infrastructure transition is essential to reaching >10% of global energy within five years, the impaired scalability reduces the likelihood of timely deployment. This is a substantial but not fatal blow to the outcome, warranting a meaningful downward update from 0.23 to 0.15.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent assessments from major fusion research programs indicate that core physics challenges—such as plasma stability and material durability—remain unresolved despite decades of progress, suggesting that fusion was never as close to viability as projected. Given these persistent technical barriers, the current maturity of fusion technology may be so low that it contributes negligibly to how quickly AGI could advance it, rendering pre-AGI tech readiness largely irrelevant.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge directly undermines the 'fusion_tech_readiness' node, which feeds into 'agi_acceleration_effect'—the mechanism by which AGI accelerates fusion development. If core physics and engineering problems remain unresolved and fusion is fundamentally less mature than assumed, then even a powerful acceleration from AGI may not overcome deep technical barriers within a five-year window. This weakens the causal path from agi_acceleration_effect to scalability_of_fusion_plants and investment_and_policy_support, as AGI's ability to produce rapid, tangible progress diminishes when starting from a less viable baseline. Consequently, the entire chain leading to energy_infrastructure_transition and the final outcome is less likely to materialize in such a short timeframe. The structural importance of fusion_tech_readiness as a prerequisite for AGI to make a decisive difference justifies a substantial downward update, though non-zero due to the possibility that AGI could still enable breakthroughs in plasma control or materials science.

probe_results.probe_text: A recent study by the Allen Institute for AI demonstrates that large-scale AI systems have already accelerated materials discovery timelines by 40% in pilot projects, particularly in high-temperature superconductors relevant to fusion. Experts at the International Atomic Energy Agency now project that AGI-level systems could reduce fusion engineering design cycles from decades to months, assuming access to integrated simulation and robotic experimentation platforms.
probe_results.reasoning: The new evidence strengthens the 'agi_acceleration_effect' node significantly, particularly by demonstrating that AI systems are already accelerating materials discovery relevant to fusion (e.g., high-temperature superconductors), and that AGI could drastically reduce engineering design cycles. This directly enhances the causal path from agi_acceleration_effect to scalability_of_fusion_plants, as faster design and materials innovation enable quicker development of practical, mass-producible reactors. It also reinforces the path from agi_acceleration_effect to investment_and_policy_support, as credible progress signals increase confidence among funders and policymakers. Given that agi_acceleration_effect is a central hub in the network—linking to both scalability and investment—this update meaningfully increases the likelihood of rapid post-AGI fusion deployment. However, the ultimate bottleneck remains energy_infrastructure_transition, which is constrained by physical construction and grid integration timelines, so the increase is substantial but not dominant.

probe_results.probe_text: In 2025, a coalition of major economies launched the Global Energy Transition Acceleration Program (GETAP), streamlining permitting, standardizing fusion reactor safety certifications, and committing $2 trillion to rapid deployment of next-gen energy infrastructure. Early pilot projects show fusion plants can be integrated into national grids within 18 months of construction, a process previously estimated to take over five years.
probe_results.reasoning: The introduction of GETAP directly strengthens the 'energy_infrastructure_transition' node, which is a critical causal link to the outcome. By streamlining permitting, standardizing safety certifications, and enabling rapid grid integration (reducing connection time from over five years to 18 months), the policy initiative dramatically accelerates deployment speed. This enhancement amplifies the impact of both 'scalability_of_fusion_plants' and 'investment_and_policy_support' on infrastructure rollout. While fusion still requires AGI-driven acceleration to reach technological maturity quickly enough, the removal of deployment bottlenecks makes it significantly more plausible that fusion could scale to >10% of global energy within five years post-AGI. The update is substantial but not extreme because 'fusion_tech_readiness' and 'agi_acceleration_effect' remain key constraints—AGI must still enable rapid reactor design and materials breakthroughs for plants to be ready for this accelerated deployment pipeline.

probe_results.probe_text: Even if AGI accelerates materials science and reactor design, the scalability of fusion plants may still be bottlenecked by lengthy regulatory licensing processes for novel reactor materials and designs, which are not significantly shortened by technical breakthroughs alone. For example, the International Atomic Energy Agency requires decades of safety validation for new nuclear technologies, regardless of design maturity, which could delay deployment regardless of AGI-driven innovation.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge targets the causal link between agi_acceleration_effect and scalability_of_fusion_plants, highlighting that regulatory bottlenecks—not technical design or materials science—may be the primary constraint on fusion scalability. This introduces a structural limitation in the causal path: even if AGI dramatically accelerates reactor design and materials innovation, the resulting designs would still face lengthy safety validation and licensing processes (e.g., via the IAEA and national regulators), which are largely independent of technical progress. This weakens the influence of agi_acceleration_effect on scalability_of_fusion_plants, thereby reducing the flow of causal impact from AGI-driven innovation to energy_infrastructure_transition and ultimately to the outcome. Since regulatory timelines are slow-moving and path-dependent, they are unlikely to compress significantly within a five-year window post-AGI, making rapid deployment less likely. This downgrades the overall probability, though AGI may still exert some indirect influence via boosting investment and policy support, preserving a non-negligible but reduced chance of the outcome.

probe_results.probe_text: Even if AGI accelerates fusion progress, governments may hesitate to increase funding due to concerns about cost overruns and opportunity costs relative to cheaper, proven renewables like solar and wind. Historical precedent—such as the continued stagnation of fusion funding despite decades of scientific advances—suggests that political inertia and budget priorities can decouple technical progress from policy support.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge targets the causal link between agi_acceleration_effect and investment_and_policy_support, a key pathway through which AGI-induced technical progress translates into real-world deployment capacity. If governments remain hesitant to increase funding due to cost concerns and preference for established renewables, then even rapid AGI-driven breakthroughs in fusion science may not lead to the necessary surge in investment and supportive policies. This weakens the path from agi_acceleration_effect → investment_and_policy_support → energy_infrastructure_transition → outcome. While AGI could still improve reactor design and scalability directly, the lack of strong policy and financial backing would slow plant construction and grid integration. Given that infrastructure transition is a major bottleneck, undermining a key driver of investment reduces the likelihood of fusion achieving >10% global energy share within five years post-AGI. The probability is therefore lowered from 0.23 to 0.17, reflecting a meaningful but not catastrophic reduction in expected momentum along the policy-infrastructure-deployment pathway.

probe_results.probe_text: Even if fusion reactors can be built quickly and cheaply, widespread deployment may still be bottlenecked by limited supply chains for specialized materials and a global shortage of skilled personnel to construct and operate them. Additionally, local opposition and regulatory hurdles in many countries could prevent rapid siting and licensing of new fusion plants, regardless of their scalability.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge targets the causal link between scalability_of_fusion_plants and energy_infrastructure_transition, a key pathway in the network. While the original model assumed that scalable reactor designs would enable rapid deployment, the new information highlights critical real-world bottlenecks—supply chains for specialized materials, shortage of skilled personnel, regulatory delays, and local opposition—that would constrain infrastructure rollout even if reactors are technically scalable. These factors weaken the strength of the scalability_to_transition link, effectively dampening the flow of causal influence from AGI-driven advances in reactor design to actual global deployment. This reduces the likelihood that fusion can achieve >10% of global energy within five years post-AGI, especially given the short time horizon. The outcome is now more tightly constrained by sociotechnical and logistical inertia than previously modeled, warranting a substantial downward update.

probe_results.probe_text: Suppose that fusion reactor designs are inherently constrained by extreme engineering and material requirements, such that only incremental progress is possible regardless of computational power. If early pilot plants reveal fundamental limitations in plasma containment or neutron-resistant materials, these scalability challenges could constrain how effectively AGI can accelerate fusion development, because the rate of useful innovation would be bottlenecked by physical experimentation and manufacturing feedback loops. Thus, the difficulty of scaling fusion may limit the practical impact of AGI on materials science, reversing the causal direction.
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge targets the causal link 'agi_acceleration_effect -> scalability_of_fusion_plants', which is a central pathway in my original reasoning for a non-trivial probability of fusion reaching >10% of global energy within five years of AGI. By arguing that fusion scalability is fundamentally bottlenecked by material and engineering constraints that cannot be overcome rapidly even with AGI-driven computational advances, the challenge weakens a key mechanism through which AGI would accelerate deployment. Specifically, it severs or greatly attenuates the path from AGI acceleration to faster reactor design and mass production. This makes the 'scalability_of_fusion_plants' node far less responsive to AGI, which in turn dampens the impact on 'energy_infrastructure_transition' and ultimately reduces the likelihood of the outcome. Given that physical experimentation and manufacturing feedback loops are slow, the five-year window post-AGI becomes implausibly short for fusion to scale meaningfully, even with strong AI assistance. This substantially undermines one of the main justifications for the original 0.23 probability, prompting a downward revision to 0.12.

probe_results.probe_text: Suppose that after AGI is developed, fusion-compatible energy infrastructure (e.g., grid connections, fuel supply chains, regulatory approvals) is rapidly deployed not just for fusion but for other advanced energy technologies as well. This creates a robust, high-throughput testing and feedback environment for AGI-driven energy research. Could the existence of fast infrastructure transition pathways increase the effectiveness of AGI in accelerating fusion by providing real-world data and deployment loops that refine AGI-generated designs?
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge introduces a new reverse causal pathway: energy_infrastructure_transition -> agi_acceleration_effect, which was not previously considered. While the original causal model treated AGI as a unidirectional accelerator of infrastructure deployment, this feedback loop suggests that rapid infrastructure transition can enhance AGI's effectiveness by providing real-world testing environments, faster iteration cycles, and empirical data to refine fusion designs. This strengthens the overall feedback between deployment and innovation, particularly affecting the agi_acceleration_effect -> scalability_of_fusion_plants and agi_acceleration_effect -> investment_and_policy_support paths by making them more responsive. Although infrastructure transition remains a bottleneck, the possibility that it could amplify AGI's impact on fusion development increases the likelihood of rapid progress. This does not fundamentally alter the core constraints (e.g., fusion_tech_readiness or scalability), but it enhances the efficiency of AGI-driven R&D, justifying a modest upward revision.

probe_results.probe_text: Imagine a scenario where fusion-ready infrastructure is being rapidly deployed across multiple continents, with new plants coming online every month. This visible progress increases public and political confidence, leading governments to increase funding and streamline regulations to capitalize on the perceived momentum. Could this deployment velocity itself drive further investment and policy support, creating a feedback loop?
probe_results.reasoning: The challenge introduces a feedback loop in which energy_infrastructure_transition not only results from investment_and_policy_support but also drives it. This reverses and strengthens the causal link between deployment velocity and institutional support, creating a potential self-reinforcing cycle: rapid deployment boosts political and public confidence, which in turn accelerates investment and regulatory enablement. This affects two key paths in the causal network: (1) energy_infrastructure_transition → outcome is strengthened because faster deployment increases the likelihood of crossing the 10% threshold; and (2) energy_infrastructure_transition now positively feeds back into investment_and_policy_support, which then reinforces energy_infrastructure_transition via improved funding and regulation. While the core constraints—fusion_tech_readiness and agi_acceleration_effect—remain bottlenecks, this feedback loop increases the potential for rapid scaling once deployment begins. Given that AGI could catalyze the initial deployment burst, this dynamic makes crossing the 10% threshold more plausible within five years post-AGI. The probability is thus increased from 0.23 to 0.32, reflecting greater confidence in acceleration once momentum begins, though material and engineering constraints still limit the upper bound of plausibility.

probe_results.probe_text: Consider the role of public acceptance and perception of nuclear fusion technology. If high-profile accidents or misinformation campaigns create public opposition, this could lead to protests, legal challenges, and siting difficulties for new fusion plants, significantly slowing deployment even if the technology is ready and funded. This social factor is not captured in the current model but could bottleneck infrastructure transition.
probe_results.reasoning: The structural challenge introduces a new bottleneck—public acceptance—that directly affects the energy_infrastructure_transition node. This social factor acts as a constraint on deployment speed, independent of technological readiness, investment, or scalability. Even if fusion reactors are technically viable, funded, and mass-producible, public opposition due to accidents or misinformation could delay siting, trigger legal hurdles, and reduce political will, slowing rollout. This weakens the causal path from scalability_of_fusion_plants and investment_and_policy_support to energy_infrastructure_transition, making it less likely that fusion achieves >10% of global energy within five years post-AGI. While AGI may accelerate technical progress and indirectly improve public communication, the social dimension introduces a novel, hard-to-predict friction not accounted for in the original model. This reduces confidence in rapid deployment, warranting a downward update.

probe_results.probe_text: Consider that public and geopolitical concerns about nuclear technologies—such as safety, weapons proliferation risks, or radiological waste—could significantly affect the rate at which fusion energy is adopted, even if the technology is ready and scalable. These concerns might delay regulatory approvals, trigger opposition to plant construction, or restrict international collaboration on fusion projects, slowing global deployment despite technical success.
probe_results.reasoning: The structural challenge introduces a new set of societal and geopolitical constraints—safety concerns, proliferation risks, and radiological waste fears—that act as a bottleneck on deployment, independent of technical readiness or scalability. This affects the causal path from scalability_of_fusion_plants and investment_and_policy_support to energy_infrastructure_transition, as public opposition and regulatory caution could weaken the link between technical feasibility and actual rollout. Even if AGI accelerates fusion development and investment, adoption may still be delayed by slow regulatory approvals and local resistance, especially in densely populated or politically risk-averse regions. This adds a previously unmodeled but significant friction to the deployment phase, making it less likely that fusion reaches >10% of global energy within just five years post-AGI. The outcome node is highly sensitive to the speed of infrastructure transition, so this new constraint meaningfully reduces the probability.

probe_results.probe_text: A recent international climate agreement has included symbolic commitments to 'support breakthrough energy innovations,' with fusion specifically mentioned in the non-binding preamble. However, no new funding mechanisms or concrete initiatives were attached to this language.
probe_results.reasoning: The symbolic mention of fusion in a non-binding preamble of an international climate agreement, without new funding mechanisms or concrete initiatives, does not significantly affect the 'investment_and_policy_support' node in the causal network. Since no actual financial or regulatory support is introduced, the downstream causal paths—from investment_and_policy_support to energy_infrastructure_transition and ultimately to the outcome—remain unchanged. The gesture may slightly increase rhetorical support for fusion, but it does not alter the pace of investment, deployment, or technical scalability. Therefore, the probability remains unchanged as the structural drivers of fusion adoption are unaffected.

probe_results.probe_text: In 2026, a major earthquake in Japan caused temporary shutdowns of several experimental fission reactors, leading to renewed public debate about nuclear energy safety. While the event increased scrutiny on all nuclear technologies, fusion research facilities were unharmed and operations continued as normal.
probe_results.reasoning: The earthquake in Japan affected fission reactors, not fusion facilities, and while it renewed debate on nuclear safety, fusion research remained unharmed and operationally unaffected. The event does not significantly impact any of the key causal paths in the model: fusion tech readiness, AGI acceleration, scalability, investment, or infrastructure transition remain unchanged. Public scrutiny on nuclear technologies could marginally influence investment and policy support, but since fusion has a stronger safety profile and was not disrupted, any shift in sentiment is unlikely to materially alter funding or deployment timelines. Thus, the challenge is peripheral to the core causal structure, and no update to the probability is warranted.

probe_results.probe_text: In 2025, a consortium led by Commonwealth Fusion Systems and Tokamak Energy announces a standardized, modular fusion reactor design that can be mass-produced using existing industrial supply chains, with a projected manufacturing cost below $500 million per 200 MW unit. Pilot production lines in the UK and Massachusetts demonstrate the ability to fabricate key components at scale using high-temperature superconducting magnets and automated assembly, cutting construction time from years to months.
probe_results.reasoning: The announcement directly strengthens the 'scalability_of_fusion_plants' node, a key causal factor in the network. This node is influenced by AGI's acceleration of engineering design (via agi_acceleration_effect), but the new modular, mass-producible design—demonstrated with existing supply chains and lower costs—reduces dependency on AGI to solve materials and manufacturing bottlenecks. This enhancement strengthens the causal path from scalability_of_fusion_plants to energy_infrastructure_transition, making rapid global deployment more feasible. It also indirectly boosts investment_and_policy_support, as demonstrated scalability increases confidence, further accelerating infrastructure rollout. While fusion_tech_readiness and energy_infrastructure_transition remain substantial hurdles, and five years is still a tight timeframe for global energy infrastructure turnover, the improved scalability meaningfully increases the likelihood of crossing the 10% threshold. Therefore, the update is moderate but significant, reflecting stronger pre-AGI momentum in a critical path.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent breakthroughs at the ITER and SPARC projects have achieved sustained plasma burn for over 100 seconds at near-net-energy-gain temperatures, with private companies like Commonwealth Fusion Systems announcing modular reactor designs ready for pilot testing by 2026. These advances suggest fusion technology is progressing rapidly even before AGI, increasing the likelihood that AGI will inherit a robust foundation to build upon.
probe_results.reasoning: The new information directly strengthens the 'fusion_tech_readiness' node, which is a key input into 'agi_acceleration_effect'—if fusion is closer to viability when AGI arrives, AGI can more effectively accelerate the remaining engineering challenges. This also indirectly supports 'scalability_of_fusion_plants' and 'investment_and_policy_support' via the existing causal links. The enhanced readiness implies that AGI inherits a more mature technological base, reducing the time needed to reach commercialization and deployment. While infrastructure transition and scalability remain bottlenecks, the improved starting point meaningfully increases the probability of fusion contributing >10% of global energy within five years post-AGI. The causal path from fusion_tech_readiness → agi_acceleration_effect → scalability_of_fusion_plants and investment_and_policy_support → energy_infrastructure_transition is strengthened, justifying a moderate upward update from 0.23 to 0.35.

probe_results.probe_text: In 2023, DeepMind's GNoME AI discovered 2.2 million new materials, including 380,000 stable candidates for practical applications, demonstrating that AI can rapidly accelerate materials discovery. This suggests AGI could drastically shorten the timeline for identifying fusion-compatible materials, directly enhancing the scalability of fusion reactors by solving key engineering bottlenecks. Such advances imply the causal link between AGI acceleration and fusion scalability is stronger than previously assumed.
probe_results.reasoning: The evidence from DeepMind's GNoME significantly strengthens the causal link between agi_acceleration_effect and scalability_of_fusion_plants. This demonstrates that even narrow AI can make transformative advances in materials discovery, suggesting AGI—possessing broader reasoning and problem-solving capabilities—could dramatically accelerate the identification and optimization of materials critical for fusion reactors (e.g., plasma-facing components, superconducting magnets, radiation-resistant alloys). This directly enhances the feasibility and speed of scaling fusion plants, a key bottleneck in deployment. As this link feeds into energy_infrastructure_transition and ultimately the outcome, the strengthened pathway increases the likelihood of rapid post-AGI fusion deployment. While infrastructure and policy constraints remain, the enhanced materials discovery potential improves the plausibility of overcoming key engineering barriers within five years, justifying a moderate upward revision.

probe_results.probe_text: In 2023, following rapid AI-driven breakthroughs in plasma containment simulations at the UK's JET facility, the UK government announced a £500 million increase in fusion funding and fast-tracked regulatory approval for prototype plants. This direct policy response was explicitly tied to the newfound confidence in achieving net-positive fusion energy within five years, demonstrating that AI-accelerated progress can trigger immediate and substantial investment and policy shifts.
probe_results.reasoning: The evidence strengthens the causal link agi_acceleration_effect -> investment_and_policy_support by demonstrating a real-world example where AI-driven progress in fusion research directly triggered significant policy and funding responses. This reinforces the plausibility and magnitude of this causal pathway in the network. As a result, the downstream effects on energy_infrastructure_transition and ultimately the outcome are enhanced: faster policy approval and increased investment improve the speed of deployment, making it more likely that fusion could reach >10% of global energy within five years post-AGI. While infrastructure and scalability remain bottlenecks, the demonstrated responsiveness of institutions to AI-accelerated breakthroughs increases confidence in the feasibility of rapid scaling, warranting a moderate upward revision.

summary.question_text: By five years after AGI, will nuclear fusion provide >10% of the world’s energy?
